title: Valid Palindrome
statement: A phrase is a palindrome if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Given a string `s`, return `true` if it is a palindrome, or `false` otherwise.

bruteForce.description: Create a new string filtering out non-alphanumeric chars and reversing it. (O(N) space).

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` (0) and `right` (N-1). While `left < right`: if `s[left]` is not alphanumeric, increment `left`. If `s[right]` is not alphanumeric, decrement `right`. If they are alphanumeric, compare their lowercase values. If they differ, return false. Otherwise, increment `left` and decrement `right`.

pythonExplanation.line: while l < r and not s[l].isalnum(): l += 1
pythonExplanation.explanation: Skip non-alphanumeric characters from left boundary pointer.
Compare character values case-insensitively, returning false on mismatch.

timeComplexity: O(N) - In the worst case, we traverse each character of string of size N at most twice.
spaceComplexity: O(1) - Constant auxiliary space is used since we modify pointers in place.
dryRun: Input: s = 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'
l = 0, r = 29. s[0]='A' is alnum. s[29]='a' is alnum. tolower('A') == tolower('a') -> l=1, r=28.
Matches and skips non-alphanumeric punctuation and spaces recursively.
Pointers meet in the middle. Result: True.
followUps: Can you write it using string slicing in Python? (Yes, `s == s[::-1]`, but that requires O(N) auxiliary space)
visualization:
s = 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'
  L=0                               R=29 -> 'A' == 'a' -> shrink
    L=1                           R=28   -> ' ' (not alnum) -> L=2
      L=2('m')                  R=28('m')-> 'm' == 'm' -> shrink
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check the `l < r` condition inside the inner while loops, leading to index-out-of-bounds crashes on strings like '...'.

title: Move Zeroes
statement: Given an integer array `nums`, move all `0`'s to the end of it while maintaining the relative order of the non-zero elements. You must do this in-place.

bruteForce.description: Copy all non-zero elements to a new array, then fill the remainder with zeros. (O(N) space).

optimized.description: Maintain a `write` pointer initialized to 0. Loop a `read` pointer through `nums`: when `nums[read]` is non-zero, swap it with `nums[write]` and increment `write`. This shifts all non-zero elements forward in order while pushing zeros back.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Check if the current element is non-zero.
Swap element at read index with element at write index to shift it forward in-place.

cppExplanation.explanation: Swap non-zero element with write index and increment write pointer.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Single pass loop over array of size N.
spaceComplexity: O(1) - Swap modifications are done in-place.
dryRun: r=1: non-zero, swap with l=0 -> [1, 0, 0, 3, 12], l=1
followUps: Can we write it by overwriting non-zeroes first, then filling zeroes? (Yes, avoids swaps)
visualization: L tracks position of next non-zero. R finds non-zeroes and swaps them to L.
commonMistakes: Copying elements to another array (violates in-place requirement).

title: Merge Sorted Array
statement: You are given two integer arrays `nums1` and `nums2`, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers `m` and `n`, representing the number of elements in `nums1` and `nums2` respectively. Merge `nums2` into `nums1` as one sorted array in-place.

bruteForce.description: Copy nums2 to the end of nums1 and run sort. (O((M+N) log(M+N)) time).

optimized.description: Start filling `nums1` from the back (at index `m + n - 1`) to avoid overwriting elements. Maintain three pointers: `p1 = m - 1` (end of active nums1), `p2 = n - 1` (end of nums2), and `p = m + n - 1` (write pointer). Compare elements and place the larger one, shifting pointers backwards. Copy leftover elements of nums2.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Initialize pointers at the active ends of the arrays, and the write index at the end of nums1.
Compare elements from the back; place the larger element at the write pointer index to keep it sorted.
If nums2 elements are left over, copy them to the beginning of nums1. (If nums1 elements are left over, they are already in correct position).

cppExplanation.explanation: Link remainder elements of nums2 to nums1 if any.

timeComplexity: O(M + N) - We perform exactly M + N comparisons and writes.
followUps: Can we merge from start? (Requires O(M) auxiliary space)
visualization:
nums1: [ 1, 2, 3, _ , _ , _ ] (p1 at 3, p at end)
nums2: [ 2, 5, 6 ] (p2 at 6)
Compare and merge from right to left.
commonMistakes: Merging from start, which overwrites values in nums1 before comparison.

title: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
statement: Given a 1-indexed array of integers `numbers` that is already sorted in non-decreasing order, find two numbers such that they add up to a specific `target` number.

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` at 1st element and `right` at last element. Calculate `sum = nums[left] + nums[right]`. If `sum == target`, return indices (1-indexed). If `sum < target`, increment `left`. If `sum > target`, decrement `right`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Convert 0-indexed values to 1-indexed indices per problem requirements.
If sum is too small, increase sum by advancing the left pointer to larger values.
If sum is too large, decrease sum by decrementing the right pointer to smaller values.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Pointers scan elements of the array at most once.
followUps: Binary search solution O(N log N)
visualization:
L --------------------> <-------------------- R
If sum is low, shift L right. If sum is high, shift R left.
commonMistakes: Not returning 1-based indices as requested by problem spec.
whenNotToUse: If array is not sorted, this two pointer logic fails (must use HashMap).

title: Container With Most Water
statement: You are given an integer array `height` of length `n`. Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container, such that the container contains the most water. Return the maximum amount of water.
pattern: Two Pointers Max Width Bounded Height

bruteForce.description: Compare all possible pairs of lines. (O(N^2) time).

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` (0) and `right` (N-1). Calculate area as `(right - left) * min(height[left], height[right])` and track the maximum. Move the pointer pointing to the shorter line inwards, since the shorter line restricts the container's height limit.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Record area: width times limiting boundary height.
Move the shorter pointer inwards. Keeping the shorter line limits capacity; we must search for a taller boundary.

cppExplanation.explanation: Calculate and update maximum area in-place.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Pointers meet in the middle in a single pass.
followUps: Can we solve this using dynamic programming? (No, state transitions are too complex)
visualization: L at 0, R at end. Move pointer with smaller height inward.
commonMistakes: Moving pointers inward randomly instead of comparing their heights.

title: 3Sum Closest
statement: Given an integer array `nums` of length `n` and an integer `target`, find three integers in `nums` such that the sum is closest to `target`. Return the sum of the three integers.

bruteForce.description: Check all triplets sums using three nested loops. (O(N^3) time).

optimized.description: Sort the array first. Loop `i` to fix the first element. Use two pointers (`left` and `right`) to find pairs that sum closest to `target - nums[i]`. Update the closest sum whenever the absolute difference `|target - current_sum|` is smaller than the previous min difference.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Update closest sum tracking if current triplet sum is closer to target than prior configurations.
If sum is smaller than target, increment left pointer to increase sum values.

cppExplanation.explanation: Move left pointer to increase values.
If sum equals target, return immediately since difference is 0 (closest possible).

timeComplexity: O(N^2) - Outer loop sweeps N items; inner two-pointer search runs in O(N).
followUps: Can we optimize this by skipping duplicate numbers? (Yes, speeds up runtime)
visualization: Sort array -> fix element i -> adjust L and R pointers to target
commonMistakes: Not taking absolute value when calculating differences.

title: Trapping Rain Water
statement: Given `n` non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.

bruteForce.description: Calculate left max and right max heights for each index using two arrays. (O(N) space).

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` (0) and `right` (n-1), and two variables to track maximum heights: `left_max` and `right_max`. Move the pointer pointing to the lower height inwards. Update the max variable, and add `max_height - current_height` to the water sum.

pythonCode:
def trap(height):
    if not height: return 0
    l, r = 0, len(height) - 1
    l_max, r_max = height[l], height[r]
    water = 0
    while l < r:
        if l_max < r_max:
            l += 1
            l_max = max(l_max, height[l])
            water += l_max - height[l]
        else:
            r -= 1
            r_max = max(r_max, height[r])
            water += r_max - height[r]
    return water

pythonExplanation.explanation: Determine which pointer to move based on the smaller bounding height.

cppExplanation.explanation: Water trapped is the difference between max limit and current bar height.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Single pass with two pointers.
followUps: Monotonic Stack solution O(N) time and space
visualization: Shifting pointers towards center while accumulating difference to bounds.
commonMistakes: Incorrect boundary pointer advancements, causing missed valleys.

title: Sort List
statement: Given the head of a linked list, return the list after sorting it in ascending order. Solve in O(N log N) time and O(1) auxiliary space.

bruteForce.description: Copy node values to an array, sort the array, and write values back. (O(N) space).

optimized.description: Use Merge Sort recursively on the linked list. 1. Find midpoint using fast/slow pointers. 2. Sever lists and recurse. 3. Merge two sorted lists using standard dummy node logic.

pythonCode:
def sortList(head):
    if not head or not head.next:
        return head
        
    # Split list using fast & slow pointers
    prev, slow, fast = None, head, head
    while fast and fast.next:
        prev = slow
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    prev.next = None # Sever connection
    
    # Recurse
    l1 = sortList(head)
    l2 = sortList(slow)
    
    # Merge
    return merge(l1, l2)

def merge(l1, l2):
    dummy = ListNode(0)
    curr = dummy
    while l1 and l2:
        if l1.val < l2.val:
            curr.next = l1
            l1 = l1.next
        else:
            curr.next = l2
            l2 = l2.next
        curr = curr.next
    curr.next = l1 or l2
    return dummy.next

pythonExplanation.explanation: Sever the linked list at the midpoint into two independent sublists.
Recursively sort the first half of the linked list.
Link remaining elements from the sorted sublists.

cppExplanation.explanation: Sever connections to split list in half.

timeComplexity: O(N log N) - Standard merge sort splitting tree height (log N) times, merging takes O(N) at each level.
spaceComplexity: O(log N) - Space to store recursion call stack frames.
followUps: Bottom-up iterative merge sort to achieve O(1) physical memory space
visualization: Find middle (fast/slow) -> sever lists -> sort recursively -> merge pointers
commonMistakes: Forgetting to sever the connection (`slow.next = None`), causing infinite recursion loops.

title: Minimum Window Substring
statement: Given two strings `s` and `t`, return the minimum window substring of `s` such that every character in `t` (including duplicates) is included. If no such substring exists, return `""`.

bruteForce.description: Verify all substring combinations sequentially. (O(M^3) time).

optimized.description: Use two pointers, `left` and `right`. Maintain a hash map of character counts in `t` and another for characters in the active window. Move `right` to expand the window. While all characters are satisfied, record the minimum length, and increment `left` to shrink the window.

pythonExplanation.explanation: If character frequency matches target count, increment matching counter.

cppExplanation.explanation: Update match constraints when shrinking left pointer.

timeComplexity: O(M + N) - Traversal and initialization checks are linear.
spaceComplexity: O(1) - Fixed mapping tables are size 128.
visualization: Adjusting pointers to scan min ranges containing target characters.
